flashcards.Back: The electromagnetic wave frequency used for wireless communication. In Wi-Fi, data is transmitted as RF signals through free space without physical cables.
A common wireless frequency range (2.400–2.4835 GHz) that offers good range and wall penetration but suffers from high interference and few non-overlapping channels.
A higher wireless frequency range (5.150–5.825 GHz) that provides more non-overlapping channels and less interference, though it has a shorter effective range than 2.4 GHz.
Specific wireless frequencies that do not interfere with each other. In the 2.4 GHz band, only channels 1, 6, and 11 are considered non-overlapping.
A human-readable text string that acts as the name of a wireless network, allowing users to identify and connect to the correct service.
The fundamental building block of an 802.11 network, consisting of a single access point and all of its associated client stations.
A wireless network architecture where multiple BSSs are interconnected by a distribution system (switched LAN), allowing clients to roam between APs while maintaining the same SSID.
A unique, machine-readable MAC address assigned to an AP's radio interface. it is used at Layer 2 to distinguish between different BSSs.
The collection of standards that define the physical layer and MAC sublayer for wireless local area networks (WLANs).
The communication mode of all standard 802.11 Wi-Fi networks, where devices can either transmit or receive on a channel, but not both simultaneously.
A wireless network configuration where all client stations communicate through a central access point rather than directly with each other.
The process where a wireless client moves its association from one access point to another within the same ESS without losing network connectivity.
Any device that contains an 802.11-compliant interface, such as a laptop, smartphone, or wireless access point.
A peer-to-peer wireless network (ad hoc mode) where two or more stations communicate directly without the use of an access point.
The process by which a wireless client station joins a BSS and establishes a logical link with an access point to gain network access.
The original, now deprecated, security standard for 802.11 networks. it is considered highly insecure due to weaknesses in its RC4-based encryption.
An intermediate security standard designed to fix WEP's flaws. it introduced TKIP for encryption but has since been superseded by WPA2 and WPA3.
A robust wireless security standard that introduced hardware-based AES encryption. it is the most widely deployed Wi-Fi security protocol today.
The latest wireless security standard that introduces SAE for better protection against brute-force attacks and mandated Protected Management Frames.
The advanced encryption mechanism used by WPA2 and WPA3 to provide data confidentiality and integrity for wireless traffic.
A legacy encryption protocol used by WPA to improve security on older hardware. it has been deprecated in favor of the much more secure AES.
An enterprise-level authentication framework that requires unique user credentials verified against a RADIUS server before granting wireless access.
The physical area covered by a single AP's radio signal, often referred to as a wireless 'cell'.
A specialized AP mode that allows wired devices without wireless capability to connect to a Wi-Fi network.
A device that receives a signal from an existing access point and retransmits it to extend the wireless coverage area.
A topology where multiple access points connect to each other wirelessly to provide coverage over a large area with minimal wired cabling.
The wired backbone infrastructure (typically Ethernet) that interconnects multiple access points to form an ESS.
The actual amount of data successfully transmitted over a wireless link, which is always lower than the theoretical bandwidth due to overhead and interference.
A measurement used to compare the strength of a desired wireless signal to the level of background radio interference.
A type of Wi-Fi antenna that radiates signal equally in all horizontal directions, typically used for general indoor coverage.
